content.text: explain: The 2D Fourier transform

# The 2D Fourier transform The **2D Fourier transform** takes an image $g(x,y)$ and rewrites it as a sum of **complex 2D plane waves**. Each plane wave has the form $e^{2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}$ — a sinusoid that ripples across the image plane in some direction at some spatial frequency. The transform tells you, for every possible $(f_x, f_y)$, *how much* of that wave is present and *where* it sits. So instead of a grid of pixel intensities, you get a grid of **complex numbers indexed by frequency**: one number per $(f_x, f_y)$. That number's **magnitude** is how strongly that wave shows up in the image, and its **phase** is where the wave is positioned (its offset). Both pieces are needed to reconstruct the image — but when we *visualize* a Fourier spectrum we usually only show $|\hat{g}|$, since phase doesn't render as anything human-readable. ## The transform pair Forward (image → spectrum): $$\hat{g}(f_x, f_y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x,y)\, e^{-2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, dx\, dy$$ Inverse (spectrum → image): $$g(x,y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{g}(f_x, f_y)\, e^{2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, df_x\, df_y$$ The minus sign in the forward transform and the plus in the inverse is just a sign convention — the two transforms are nearly identical, which is one reason Fourier theory is so symmetric. ## What one Fourier-space pixel actually represents This is the bit that confuses most people on the first pass, so it's worth pinning down: - **One pixel in Fourier space depends on every pixel in the original image.** That's because the integral runs over all $(x,y)$. - **Conversely, one pixel in real space contributes to every pixel in Fourier space.** Each spatial sample is built from a contribution at every frequency. So Fourier space is **not** a spatial map. A bright spot at $(f_x, f_y) = (0.1, 0)$ doesn't mean "something interesting happens at that location" — it means *the image as a whole contains a strong horizontal stripe pattern at frequency 0.1 cycles per pixel*. ## The frequency grid and `fftshift` The discrete FFT returns frequencies in a peculiar order. For $N = 7$ samples, the raw FFT bins come out as $$[0,\; \tfrac{1}{7},\; \tfrac{2}{7},\; \tfrac{3}{7},\; -\tfrac{3}{7},\; -\tfrac{2}{7},\; -\tfrac{1}{7}]$$ — DC first, then positive frequencies climbing, then negative frequencies wrapping back. After applying `fftshift` they get reordered into the natural, monotonic layout $$[-\tfrac{3}{7},\; -\tfrac{2}{7},\; -\tfrac{1}{7},\; 0,\; \tfrac{1}{7},\; \tfrac{2}{7},\; \tfrac{3}{7}].$$ In 2D this puts the **DC term in the centre of the spectrum image**, which is the layout every spectrum visualization assumes. If you ever look at a Fourier image and the bright spot is in the corner instead of the middle, you forgot the shift. ## Radial structure: why spectra look like ringed blobs Frequencies with the same magnitude $$|f| = \sqrt{f_x^2 + f_y^2}$$ form **concentric rings** in Fourier space. This gives a clean physical interpretation: - **Centre = low frequencies.** Slow variations: overall brightness, large-scale shapes, gradients. - **Outer rings = high frequencies.** Edges, fine detail, texture, noise. Natural images have a famous statistical regularity: the **average magnitude on each ring decays roughly as $1/\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$**. The DC term (the average pixel value) is enormous, and energy falls off quickly as you move outward. That's why if you `imshow` a raw spectrum it looks like a single white dot in a black void — the centre saturates the colormap. The fix is to plot $\log(A+1)$ instead, which compresses the dynamic range and reveals the structure. ## Pipeline view ```mermaid flowchart LR img(["image g(x,y)"]) -->|"FFT2"| raw["raw spectrum
(DC in corner)"] raw -->|"fftshift"| spec(["ĝ(f_x, f_y)
DC in centre"]) spec -->|"|·|, log"| vis["log-magnitude image
concentric rings visible"] spec -->|"phase"| ph["phase image
(rarely visualized)"] classDef out fill:#e3f2fd class spec out ``` ## Why this matters The 2D FT is a foundation, not an end in itself. Once you have it, several spatial-domain operations become **multiplication of complex numbers in Fourier space**, which is faster, cleaner, and exposes structure that's invisible in pixel space. The downstream tools the lecture builds on this base — [[low-pass-filter]], [[high-pass-filter]], [[band-pass-filter]], the [[convolution-theorem]], the [[fourier-shift-theorem]], spectrum-based [[fourier-resizing]], [[deconvolution]], [[wiener-deconvolution]], and the [[nyquist-theorem]] — all assume you've internalized the 2D FT first. --- *The lecture frames the 2D FT as a direct generalisation of the 1D case: the integral $\hat{g}(f_x, f_y) = \int g(x,y)\, e^{-2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, dx\, dy$ decomposes the image into plane waves, with each Fourier-space pixel being one complex number depending on **all** pixels of the input image, and each input pixel contributing to **all** Fourier pixels. The slides emphasise the `fftshift` convention (centring DC), the radial reading of the spectrum (low frequency at centre, high frequency at edge), and the $1/\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$ falloff that motivates log-magnitude display. Source: `10_fourier_transform2.pdf`, slides 1–7; see [[fourier-transform-2d]] and `10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/lecture-10-fourier-transform-2.md`.* **Remember this:** The 2D FT writes $g(x,y)$ as a sum of complex plane waves $e^{2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}$; each Fourier-space pixel is one complex number (magnitude + phase) encoding how strongly one frequency $(f_x, f_y)$ is present across the *entire* image. **Beyond the lecture:** I emphasised the "phase encodes position, magnitude encodes strength" framing more explicitly than the slides do — a useful intuition is that **phase carries most of the perceptual content of an image** (if you swap two images' magnitudes but keep their phases, you'll still recognise each image from its phase). The slides mention the $1/|f|$ falloff but don't connect it to the broader fact that this is a **statistical property of natural images** (the so-called $1/f$ spectrum), studied heavily in computational neuroscience and image-prior modelling. Neither point is required for the exam, but they're the "why" behind the conventions.
I dont get it. explain it to me like im a baby

OK, baby mode. Forget the integrals. ## The core idea: every image is a stack of stripes Imagine you have a recipe. A smoothie can be broken down into "30% banana, 50% strawberry, 20% milk." If I hand you a smoothie, you could (in principle) reverse-engineer the recipe. The **Fourier transform does the same thing for images**, except the "ingredients" are not fruits — they're **striped wave patterns**. Every image you can imagine — a face, a cat, a chessboard, a noisy mess — can be built by stacking up enough striped patterns and adding them together. The Fourier transform's job is to **figure out the recipe**: how much of each stripe pattern is in your image. ## What does a "stripe pattern" look like? A stripe pattern has two things going on: 1. **A direction** — vertical stripes, horizontal stripes, diagonal stripes, anything in between. 2. **A spacing** — fat stripes (low frequency) or thin stripes (high frequency). That's it. Every Fourier "ingredient" is just a pattern of light-to-dark-to-light bands going in some direction with some thickness. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["fat horizontal stripes
(low freq, vertical direction)"] --> M["+"] B["thin vertical stripes
(high freq, horizontal direction)"] --> M C["medium diagonal stripes"] --> M D["...thousands more..."] --> M M --> IMG(["your image"]) ``` If you mix the right amounts of the right stripes, you get back the original image. **Exactly.** No approximation, no information lost. ## Why two numbers $(f_x, f_y)$? Because a stripe pattern has a direction, you need **two numbers** to describe it: - $f_x$ = how fast the pattern wiggles as you move **left-right** across the image. - $f_y$ = how fast it wiggles as you move **up-down**. If $f_x$ is big and $f_y = 0$ → thin **vertical stripes** (the pattern wiggles fast left-right, doesn't change as you go up-down). If $f_y$ is big and $f_x = 0$ → thin **horizontal stripes**. If both are equal → **diagonal** stripes. So the pair $(f_x, f_y)$ picks out one specific stripe pattern. **The Fourier transform tells you "how much of this stripe pattern is in my image"** for every possible $(f_x, f_y)$. ## The "one Fourier pixel = whole image" thing This is the part you said you don't get, so let's do it slowly. When you ask the question *"how much of this thin-vertical-stripe pattern is in my picture of a cat?"* — you can't answer that by looking at one corner of the cat. You have to scan the **entire image**, see how well that stripe pattern lines up with what's there, and add it all up. **One number out — but the input was the whole image.** That's all the lecture means by "one pixel in Fourier space depends on all pixels in real space." It's not weird. It's just: you can't measure a global pattern by peeking at one spot. ## What does the spectrum picture actually show? When you see those black images with a bright cross or a glowing centre — that's a **map of the recipe**. Each spot in that map is "how much of one stripe pattern is in the image." - **Centre of the map** = fat, slow stripes (the big-picture stuff: overall brightness, large shapes). - **Edges of the map** = thin, fast stripes (fine details, edges, noise). - **Bright spot** = "lots of this stripe pattern is in the image." - **Dark spot** = "barely any of this stripe pattern." Most natural images have a really bright **centre** (lots of slow variation — the average colour, the rough shapes) and dim outer rings (less fine detail than coarse structure). That's the famous "$1/f$ falloff" — and it's why the centre saturates and you have to plot $\log$ of the magnitude to see anything else. ## Why anyone bothers Once you have your image written as a recipe of stripes, a bunch of operations get **way easier**: - Want to **blur** the image? Throw away the thin-stripe ingredients (high frequencies) and rebuild. Done. - Want to **sharpen** it? Boost the thin-stripe ingredients. - Want to **shift** it sideways? You don't even have to move pixels — just nudge the recipe. You'll see all of this in the rest of lecture 10. But the core unlock is: **stop thinking of an image as a grid of pixels, start thinking of it as a recipe of stripes.** --- *The lecture's actual framing: $\hat{g}(f_x, f_y) = \int g(x,y)\, e^{-2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, dx\, dy$ — each $(f_x, f_y)$ gives one complex number; one Fourier pixel depends on all real-space pixels and vice versa; `fftshift` puts DC at centre; magnitudes on rings of equal $|f| = \sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$ decay roughly as $1/|f|$ in natural images. Source: `10_fourier_transform2.pdf`, slides 1–7.* **Remember this:** Every image = a recipe of striped wave patterns; the 2D Fourier transform tells you how much of each stripe pattern (one per direction-and-thickness combo) is in your image. **Beyond the lecture:** The "stripes" framing is mine — the slides go straight to plane-wave equations. The mental model is the same thing, just dressed down. Also, "complex number" really just means "magnitude + phase" = "how much of the stripe is there + where the stripe's peaks land" — phase being the "where" is the part the equations hide behind $e^{i\theta}$.

For every lecture note in 10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/, add a clickable link to the source slide PDF. Process:

For each lecture note, read the frontmatter source: field to get the PDF filename
Insert this line immediately after the H1 title (the # Lecture NN — ... line):
markdown📑 [Open slides ↗](../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf)
Replace {filename} with the actual PDF filename from frontmatter (preserving any (1) suffixes etc.).
If the line already exists in the note, skip — don't duplicate.
Leave one blank line between the title, the link, and the next section.

Path resolves correctly: `lectures/` → `../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/`.

A couple of clarifications before I roll this out to all 11:

1. **Replace or coexist?** Are you scrapping Option A (the inline H1 link) entirely, or do you want both the frontmatter property *and* the inline link? My read is "replace" — confirm.
2. **Vault-root wikilink path** — I'll use `[[30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/...]]` exactly as in your example (Obsidian resolves it from the vault root regardless of the note's location, so this works from `10-Courses/...`).
3. **Property placement** — I'll put `slides:` right after `lecture:` and before `created:` to match your example exactly.

Say **go** (and clarify replace-vs-coexist if applicable) and I'll process all 11.

replace

   - Sections: Overview, Key concepts (linked), Equations, Diagrams, Open questions

content.text: Done. The slide ingest workflow now requires the `slides:` frontmatter property on every new lecture note, with the wikilink format and filename rules spelled out.

Right now those citations are plain text in square brackets — Markdown sees them as nothing special. To make them clickable and jump to the right slide, you need to wrap them in actual link syntax with the PDF page fragment.

## The fix

Change this:

```
[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]
```

To this:

```
[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf](../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=7)
```

Same visible text, but it's now a real link. Click it → Obsidian opens the PDF at page 7 in a new pane.

## Update CLAUDE.md

The current instruction is being interpreted as "produce text that *looks like* a citation." You need to make the link syntax explicit and required. Replace your slide-link instructions with this:

```bash
cat << 'EOF'

### Inline source citations — must be clickable links, not plain text

Every slide citation in an explanation must be a real Markdown link with a `#page=N` fragment, not bracketed plain text.

**Wrong** (plain text, not clickable):
```
[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]
```

**Right** (Markdown link with PDF page fragment):
```
[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf](../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=7)
```

The visible text stays the same; what's different is the `(...)` link target with the `#page=N` fragment. Clicking opens the PDF at the cited slide.

**Path rules:**

- The path is relative to the file containing the link
- Lecture notes live at `10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/`, so they reach the PDF folder via `../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf`
- Concept notes live at `20-Concepts/`, so they reach via `../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf`
- When answering in chat (no fixed file location), use the vault-root-absolute path: `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf` — it works in chat output because Obsidian resolves it from vault root

**Citation patterns:**

- Single slide: `[slide 14 of 12_feature_detectors2.pdf](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf#page=14)`
- Range: link the first page in the range with descriptive text covering the range — `[slides 14–17 of 12_feature_detectors2.pdf](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf#page=14)`
- Multiple non-contiguous: stack two links — `[slide 14](path#page=14) [slide 22](path#page=22)`

**Filename rules:**

- Use the EXACT PDF filename including any `(1)` suffix — e.g. `13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf`
- Do not strip extensions, do not URL-encode parentheses (Obsidian handles them)
- Do not invent slide numbers — verify by checking `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/slides-png/{lecture}/slide-NN.png` if uncertain

**For concept and lecture notes**, prefer wikilinks: `[[hough-transform]]`, `[[lecture-12-feature-detectors-2#Hough Transform]]`. These don't need the page fragment because they're not PDFs.

The source's exact framing section at the end of an explanation should also use a clickable link — the verbatim quote should be followed by a clickable slide reference, not plain text.
EOF
```

Append that to your existing CLAUDE.md, and replace any conflicting older instructions about slide links.

## A test prompt to verify it took

Ask:

> Grounded explanation: explain the Nyquist theorem and aliasing. All slide citations must be clickable Markdown links with `#page=N` fragments. After your answer, list every clickable link you produced.

The answer should have inline `[slide N of foo.pdf](path/foo.pdf#page=N)` formatted citations. Click one — does it open the PDF at the right page? If yes, you're done.

If the slide opens at the wrong page, your slide-number-to-page-number mapping is off for that specific PDF (some decks have title pages that throw off the count). Quick check:

```bash
pdfinfo "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf" | grep Pages
ls "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/30-Sources/Computer-Vision/slides-png/10_fourier_transform2" | wc -l
```

Same number = slide N maps to page N. Off by one = there's a title page; tell Claude in CLAUDE.md to add 1 to the slide number when generating the link for that lecture.

## Retroactively fix existing notes

If you want all the explanations Claude has already written to have working links (including the one you just pasted me), tell Claude:

> Find every plain-text slide citation in `10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/`, `20-Concepts/`, and any saved chat notes. They look like `[slide N of filename.pdf]`. Convert each to a clickable Markdown link: `[slide N of filename.pdf](../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/filename.pdf#page=N)` — adjusting the relative path based on where the file lives. Show me the diff for one file before applying.

That'll backfill clickability across everything you've generated so far.

## One small caveat

Obsidian renders the link as the text inside `[...]`. So `[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf](path)` shows up as the whole filename string, which is verbose. If you'd rather see just `[slide 7]` displayed, change the visible text:

```markdown
[slide 7](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=7)
```

Cleaner reading, but you lose the filename at a glance. Trade-off — your call. Tell Claude in CLAUDE.md which you prefer:

> Use compact citation text: `[slide 7]` rather than `[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]` when the surrounding context already makes the lecture clear. Use the long form only when you're citing across multiple lectures in the same paragraph.

Run the test prompt and tell me whether the links work. If they do, do the retroactive fix on your existing content.
I'll do this in three steps: update CLAUDE.md, verify slide-to-page mapping across lectures, then run the Nyquist test. Then handle the retroactive fix.

content.newText:
- Use markdown freely where it helps: **bold** for key terms, *italics* for emphasis or asides, equations in `$...$` and `$$...$$`, numbered/bulleted lists for steps or enumerations, Mermaid diagrams for flows or architectures.
- **No `> blockquote` formatting in the body** — it fragments the flow. Save quoted source text for the dedicated section at the end.

**Inline source citations — must be clickable links with `#page=N`, not plain text:**

Every slide citation must be a real Markdown link with a `#page=N` fragment so it opens the PDF at the cited page. **Bracketed plain text without a link target is broken** — Markdown renders it as nothing special.

**Wrong** (looks like a citation, isn't clickable):
```
[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]
```

**Right** (real Markdown link with PDF page fragment):
```
[slide 7](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=7)
```

**Path rules** (the `(...)` target depends on where the link lives):

- Lecture notes (`10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/`) → `../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N`
- Concept notes (`20-Concepts/`) → `../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N`
- Chat output (no fixed file location) → vault-root-absolute: `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N`

**Citation patterns:**

- Single slide: `[slide 14](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf#page=14)`
- Slide range: link the first page with descriptive text covering the range — `[slides 14–17](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf#page=14)`
- Multiple non-contiguous slides: stack two links — `[slide 14](path#page=14) [slide 22](path#page=22)`
- Synthesis across many slides: `[synthesis of slides 14–22](path#page=14)`
- Lecture note section: `[[lecture-12-feature-detectors-2#Hough Transform]]` (wikilink — no `#page=`, it's not a PDF)
- Concept note: `[[hough-transform]]` (wikilink)
- Mixing: stack them — `[slide 14](path#page=14) [[hough-transform]]`

**Compact text preferred.** Use `[slide 7]` as the visible text when the surrounding paragraph already makes the lecture clear. Use the long form `[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]` only when citing across multiple lectures in the same paragraph and the filename is needed for disambiguation.

**Filename rules:**

- Use the EXACT PDF filename, including any `(1)` suffix — e.g. `13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf`. Don't strip `.pdf`, don't URL-encode parentheses (Obsidian handles them).
- Don't invent slide numbers — verify by checking the matching `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png` if uncertain.
- Slide N usually maps to PDF page N, but title pages can shift the mapping by 1. If a link opens at the wrong page, check `pdfinfo` page count against the slides-png count for that lecture.

If you can identify the slide number, ALWAYS prefer the slide reference — it's the most authoritative source. Concept notes were derived from slides; the slides are the original.

If a claim has no source support, mark it inline as `[not in source]` rather than skipping it. Never invent specifics that weren't in the source.

**End every grounded answer with three short sections:**

- Use markdown freely where it helps: **bold** for key terms, *italics* for emphasis or asides, equations in `$...$` and `$$...$$`, numbered/bulleted lists for steps or enumerations, Mermaid diagrams for flows or architectures.
- **No `> blockquote` formatting in the body** — it fragments the flow. Save quoted source text for the dedicated section at the end.

**Inline source markers — be specific:**

After each substantive claim, add an inline marker pointing to the exact source location. Use these formats:

- Lecture slide: `[slide 14 of 12_feature_detectors2.pdf](../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf)` — when the claim is anchored to a specific slide
- A range: `[slides 14-17 of 12_feature_detectors2.pdf]` — when the explanation spans multiple slides
- Lecture note section: `[[lecture-12-feature-detectors-2#Hough Transform]]` — when the claim is grounded in your own lecture note's specific section
- Concept note: `[[hough-transform]]` — when the claim summarizes an atomic concept note
- Multiple sources: stack them — `[slide 14 of 12_feature_detectors2.pdf] [[hough-transform]]`

If you can identify the slide number, ALWAYS prefer the slide reference — it's the most authoritative source. Concept notes were derived from slides; the slides are the original.

If you cannot pin a claim to a specific slide because the lecture spreads it across multiple slides or it's a synthesis, use a slide range or note that explicitly: `[synthesis of slides 14-22 of 12_feature_detectors2.pdf]`.

If a claim has no source support, mark it inline as `[not in source]` rather than skipping it. Never invent specifics that weren't in the source.

**End every grounded answer with three short sections:**

rawInput.new_string:
**Inline source citations — must be clickable links with `#page=N`, not plain text:**

Every slide citation must be a real Markdown link with a `#page=N` fragment so it opens the PDF at the cited page. **Bracketed plain text without a link target is broken** — Markdown renders it as nothing special.

**Wrong** (looks like a citation, isn't clickable):
```
[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]
```

**Right** (real Markdown link with PDF page fragment):
```
[slide 7](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=7)
```

**Path rules** (the `(...)` target depends on where the link lives):

- Lecture notes (`10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/`) → `../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N`
- Concept notes (`20-Concepts/`) → `../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N`
- Chat output (no fixed file location) → vault-root-absolute: `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N`

**Citation patterns:**

- Single slide: `[slide 14](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf#page=14)`
- Slide range: link the first page with descriptive text covering the range — `[slides 14–17](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf#page=14)`
- Multiple non-contiguous slides: stack two links — `[slide 14](path#page=14) [slide 22](path#page=22)`
- Synthesis across many slides: `[synthesis of slides 14–22](path#page=14)`
- Lecture note section: `[[lecture-12-feature-detectors-2#Hough Transform]]` (wikilink — no `#page=`, it's not a PDF)
- Concept note: `[[hough-transform]]` (wikilink)
- Mixing: stack them — `[slide 14](path#page=14) [[hough-transform]]`

**Compact text preferred.** Use `[slide 7]` as the visible text when the surrounding paragraph already makes the lecture clear. Use the long form `[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]` only when citing across multiple lectures in the same paragraph and the filename is needed for disambiguation.

**Filename rules:**

- Use the EXACT PDF filename, including any `(1)` suffix — e.g. `13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf`. Don't strip `.pdf`, don't URL-encode parentheses (Obsidian handles them).
- Don't invent slide numbers — verify by checking the matching `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png` if uncertain.
- Slide N usually maps to PDF page N, but title pages can shift the mapping by 1. If a link opens at the wrong page, check `pdfinfo` page count against the slides-png count for that lecture.

If you can identify the slide number, ALWAYS prefer the slide reference — it's the most authoritative source. Concept notes were derived from slides; the slides are the original.

If a claim has no source support, mark it inline as `[not in source]` rather than skipping it. Never invent specifics that weren't in the source.

**End every grounded answer with three short sections:**

1. **The source's exact framing** — one short paragraph in *italics* (no blockquote), capturing how the lecture actually phrases the concept. End with a clickable slide link, e.g. `*"verbatim or near-verbatim text"* — [slide 14 of 12_feature_detectors2.pdf](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf#page=14)`. The trailing citation must be a real Markdown link with `#page=N`, not plain text.

2. **Remember this** — one-line atomic fact for flashcards.

content.text: Now verifying slide-to-page mapping for all 11 lectures.

content.title:
cd /Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/30-Sources/Computer-Vision && for pdf in pdf/*.pdf; do
  name=$(basename "$pdf" .pdf)
  pages=$(pdfinfo "$pdf" 2>/dev/null | awk '/^Pages:/ {print $2}')
  if [ -d "slides-png/$name" ]; then
    pngs=$(find "slides-png/$name" -maxdepth 1 -name 'slide-*.png' | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
  else
    pngs="(no dir)"
  fi
  printf "%-55s pages=%-4s pngs=%s\n" "$name" "$pages" "$pngs"
done

  if [ -d "slides-png/$name" ]; then

  else

  fi
  printf "%-55s pages=%-4s pngs=%s\n" "$name" "$pages" "$pngs"
done

content.text:
All 11 lectures have 1:1 page↔slide mapping (slide N = PDF page N). No off-by-one corrections needed. ## Test prompt: Nyquist theorem + aliasing The **Nyquist theorem** sets the minimum sampling rate needed to faithfully digitize a continuous signal: if the highest frequency present in the signal is $u_{\max}$, you must sample at a rate of at least $2 u_{\max}$ samples per unit length, or equivalently your sample spacing $x_0$ must satisfy $$u_{\max} \leq \frac{1}{2 x_0}$$ [slide 48 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=48). Sample any slower than that, and high-frequency content gets misinterpreted as low-frequency content — the phenomenon called **aliasing**. The Fourier-domain picture makes the *why* obvious. Sampling a continuous signal at spacing $x_0$ is mathematically equivalent to multiplying it by an impulse train of period $x_0$. By the [[convolution-theorem]], multiplication in real space is convolution in frequency space, and the Fourier transform of an impulse train is itself an impulse train — but with spacing $1/x_0$. So the spectrum of the sampled signal is the original spectrum **copied and pasted at every multiple of $1/x_0$** [slides 46–48](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=46). If those copies don't overlap, you can recover the original by isolating one copy (low-pass filter). If they do overlap, the overlapping high-frequency tails get added into the low-frequency region of the next copy — and once they're added, you can't separate them again. That irreversible mixing is aliasing. The classic visual is the moiré pattern on the photographed building [slide 45](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=45) — fine architectural detail with a frequency above $1/(2x_0)$ shows up in the photo as bogus low-frequency wavy bands. The "non-overlap" condition is exactly Nyquist: spacing-$1/x_0$ copies of a spectrum supported on $|u| \leq u_{\max}$ don't overlap iff $1/x_0 \geq 2 u_{\max}$, i.e. $u_{\max} \leq 1/(2x_0)$. ```mermaid flowchart LR cont["continuous F(u),
support |u| ≤ u_max"] -->|"× impulse train period x₀"| sampled["F_s(u): copies spaced 1/x₀"] sampled --> q{"1/x₀ ≥ 2 u_max?"} q -->|"yes"| ok["copies don't overlap → recoverable"] q -->|"no"| alias["overlap → aliasing
(moiré on the building)"] ``` The practical move: if you're going to downsample, **low-pass filter first** to chop off frequencies above the new Nyquist limit. Otherwise those frequencies don't disappear — they reappear as fake low-frequency artefacts. --- *The lecture frames it as: copies of the spectrum get stamped down at every multiple of the sampling frequency $1/x_0$; if $1/x_0 < 2 u_{\max}$ the copies overlap and aliasing is irreversible. The Nyquist bound is then read directly off the non-overlap condition: $u_{\max} \leq 1/(2 x_0)$. Source: [slide 48 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=48).* **Remember this:** Sample at $\geq 2 u_{\max}$ or the spectrum copies overlap and high frequencies masquerade as low ones (aliasing) — irreversibly. **Beyond the lecture:** The "low-pass before downsampling" practical recipe was implicit in the slides (the spectrum-crop downsampling on [slide 29](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=29) is exactly this — it's an anti-aliased downsample by construction). Worth knowing explicitly because in real-world pipelines, naive `[::2]`-style decimation aliases unless you smooth first. ### Every link produced (for verification) 1. `[slide 48 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=48)` 2. `[slides 46–48](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=46)` 3. `[slide 45](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=45)` 4. `[[convolution-theorem]]` (wikilink, no #page) 5. `[slide 48 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=48)` (in source-framing) 6. `[slide 29](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=29)` Vault-root paths verified: PDF exists at `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf`, page count = 49, so all referenced pages (29, 45, 46, 48) are valid. --- Click one of the links above. If it opens the PDF at the right slide in Obsidian, say **go retro** and I'll backfill all existing lecture/concept notes (and the previous Fourier-transform answer if you want it saved). If the page is off by one for some specific lecture, tell me which and I'll add a per-lecture offset rule.
